questions.question: Let X and Y denote the sets containing 2 and 20 distinct objects respectively and F denote the set of all possible functions defined from X to Y. Let f be randomly chosen from F. The probability of f being one-to-one is ______.
questions.to_find: The probability that a function chosen uniformly at random from X to Y is one-to-one

understand.plain: Here X has 2 elements and Y has 20 elements, and F is the set of ALL functions from X to Y. Picking f at random means every function in F is equally likely, so the probability of a property is (number of functions with that property) / (total number of functions). The total number of functions from a 2-element set to a 20-element set is 20^2 = 400. A one-to-one function must send the 2 distinct inputs to 2 DISTINCT outputs: 20 choices for the first input and 19 for the second, giving 20 x 19 = 380. So the probability is 380/400 = 0.95.

keywords.explain: Each of the |X| inputs independently chooses any of the |Y| outputs, so the count of all functions is |Y| raised to the power |X|.
keywords.example: From 2 inputs to 20 outputs there are 20^2 = 400 functions.

keywords.explain: Distinct inputs map to distinct outputs; with 2 inputs this means the two images differ.
keywords.example: 20 choices for f(x_1) then 19 for f(x_2) gives 20 x 19 = 380 injections.

keywords.explain: When every outcome in the sample space is equally likely, probability = favorable outcomes / total outcomes.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='518' height='208' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='40' class='lbl ink'>|X| = 2 , |Y| = 20</text><text x='20' y='80' class='lbl ink'>Total functions = 20^2 = 400</text><text x='20' y='120' class='lbl ink'>One-to-one = 20 x 19 = 380</text><text x='20' y='165' class='lbl hi' style='fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'>P = 380 / 400</text><text x='20' y='200' class='big ok'>= 0.95</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Total functions 20^2 = 400, injective functions 20 x 19 = 380, so probability = 380/400 = 0.95.

given.aim: Find the probability that a uniformly random function from X (2 elements) to Y (20 elements) is one-to-one.

terms.meaning: Domain set with |X| = 2 distinct objects
terms.connects: Its 2 elements become the exponent in the total count and the number of injective choice stages

terms.meaning: Codomain set with |Y| = 20 distinct objects
terms.connects: Supplies 20 image options, decreasing as they are used for injectivity

terms.meaning: The set of all functions from X to Y (the sample space)
terms.connects: Its size is the denominator of the probability

terms.meaning: A function sending the 2 inputs to 2 distinct outputs
terms.connects: Its count 20 x 19 = 380 is the numerator

given.plan: Count total functions (20^2) and injective functions (20 x 19) separately, then divide to get the equally-likely probability.

steps.title: Count total and injective functions
steps.apply: All functions: each of the 2 inputs picks any of 20 images, so |F| = 20^2 = 400. Injective functions: the first input has 20 image choices and the second must avoid it, leaving 19, so 20 x 19 = 380 = P(20,2).
steps.note: Injective count is the permutation P(20,2); total count allows repeats.

steps.title: Divide favorable by total
steps.apply: Since f is chosen uniformly from F, P(one-to-one) = 380/400 = 19/20 = 0.95.

solution.result: The probability that the random function is one-to-one is 380/400 = 0.95.

questions.formula_note: Divide injective count P(20,2) = 380 by total count 20^2 = 400 (probability-equally-likely) to get 0.95; the key is using |Y|^|X| for the denominator, not |X|^|Y|.

questions.question: A function f, defined on the set of positive integers N+, satisfies the following properties: f(n) = f(n/2) if n is even; f(n) = f(n+5) if n is odd. Let R = {i | exists j: f(j) = i} be the set of distinct values that f takes. The maximum possible size of R is ___________.
questions.to_find: The maximum possible number of distinct values the function f can take

understand.plain: The two rules force certain inputs to share the same f-value: an even n is tied to n/2, and an odd n is tied to n+5. Following these links partitions the positive integers into groups that must all map to one common value; the number of distinct values R can have is at most the number of such groups. The decisive invariant is divisibility by 5: halving an even multiple of 5 keeps it a multiple of 5, and adding 5 to an odd number keeps its 'multiple-of-5 or not' status. So every positive integer collapses into just two groups -- multiples of 5, and non-multiples of 5 -- giving at most 2 distinct values, hence the maximum size of R is 2.

keywords.explain: Each rule f(n) = f(something) chains many inputs to one shared value; tracing the chains groups the integers into equivalence classes.
keywords.example: f(8) = f(4) = f(2) = f(1), so 1,2,4,8 share a value.

keywords.explain: Halving (when even) and adding 5 (when odd) never turn a non-multiple of 5 into a multiple of 5 or vice versa, so the two kinds of numbers can never mix.
keywords.example: 5 -> 10 -> 5 -> ... stays in multiples of 5; 3 -> 8 -> 4 -> 2 -> 1 stays non-multiple.

keywords.explain: R is the image of f; its size equals the number of independent value-groups, which here is at most the number of equivalence classes = 2.
keywords.example: One value for multiples of 5, another for the rest.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='538' height='218' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='40' class='lbl ink'>Class 1 (not mult. of 5): 1,2,3,4,6,7,8,9 ...</text><text x='20' y='80' class='lbl ink'>Class 2 (mult. of 5): 5,10,15,20,25 ...</text><text x='20' y='125' class='sub mut'>even: f(n)=f(n/2)   odd: f(n)=f(n+5)</text><text x='20' y='160' class='lbl ink'>2 classes => at most 2 distinct values</text><text x='20' y='205' class='big ok'>max |R| = 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The rules split positive integers into two classes -- multiples of 5 and non-multiples -- so f can take at most 2 distinct values.

given.aim: Determine the largest possible number of distinct values taken by f given the two recurrence-style rules.

terms.meaning: A function on positive integers constrained by the two rules
terms.example: f(1), f(2), ... are values to be determined up to the constraints
terms.connects: Its image is exactly the set R

terms.meaning: f(n) = f(n/2), tying n to its half
terms.connects: Links each even number downward toward an odd number

terms.meaning: f(n) = f(n+5), tying an odd n to n+5
terms.connects: Bridges odd numbers to even numbers, continuing the chain

terms.meaning: The set of distinct values f takes (its image)
terms.connects: Equals the number of equivalence classes formed by the rules

given.plan: Trace the forced equalities to group all positive integers, using the divisibility-by-5 invariant to show there are exactly two groups, so |R| <= 2 with equality achievable.

steps.title: Chain inputs into equivalence classes
steps.formula_raw: f(n) = f(target(n)) for all n => inputs in one chain share one value
steps.apply: Reduce any n: while even, halve it (f unchanged); while odd, add 5 then it becomes even and halve again. For example 3 -> 8 -> 4 -> 2 -> 1, so f(3)=f(8)=f(4)=f(2)=f(1). All numbers linked this way must share a single f-value.
steps.note: The number of distinct values cannot exceed the number of such chains (classes).

steps.title: Show exactly two classes via the mod-5 invariant
steps.formula_raw: even halving and odd +5 preserve 'divisible by 5?' => 2 classes
steps.apply: If 5 | n and n is even, then 5 | (n/2); if n is odd then 5 | (n+5) iff 5 | n. So multiples of 5 stay multiples of 5 and non-multiples stay non-multiples -- the two kinds never merge. Within each kind every number reduces to a common representative (1 for non-multiples, 5 for multiples), so there are exactly 2 classes. Assigning different values to the two classes is consistent, so the maximum |R| = 2.
steps.note: Both classes are non-empty and independent, so 2 distinct values are actually attainable.

solution.result: The rules collapse all positive integers into 2 classes (multiples vs non-multiples of 5), so the maximum possible size of R is 2.

questions.formula_note: Forced equalities partition N+ into equivalence classes (function-image-equivalence-classes); the divisibility-by-5 invariant yields exactly 2 classes, so max |R| = 2.

questions.question: Consider the following sets, where n >= 2: S_1: Set of all n x n matrices with entries from the set {a, b, c}. S_2: Set of all functions from the set {0, 1, 2, ..., n^2 - 1} to the set {0, 1, 2}. Which of the following choice(s) is/are correct? A. There does not exist a bijection from S_1 to S_2. B. There exists a surjection from S_1 to S_2. C. There exists a bijection from S_1 to S_2. D. There does not exist an injection from S_1 to S_2.
questions.to_find: Which statements about maps between S_1 and S_2 are correct

understand.plain: First count both sets. An n x n matrix has n^2 entries, each chosen from a 3-element set {a,b,c}, so |S_1| = 3^(n^2). The domain {0,1,...,n^2-1} has exactly n^2 elements and the codomain {0,1,2} has 3 elements, so the number of functions is |S_2| = 3^(n^2). The two sets have EQUAL finite cardinality, |S_1| = |S_2|. Whenever two finite sets have the same size, there is a bijection between them, and a bijection is automatically both a surjection and an injection. So C (bijection exists) and B (surjection exists) are correct, while A (no bijection) and D (no injection) are false.

keywords.explain: Each of the n^2 matrix entries independently takes one of 3 values, so the count is 3 raised to n^2.
keywords.example: For n = 2 there are 3^4 = 81 such matrices.

keywords.explain: A function from a set of size n^2 to a set of size 3 is counted by |codomain|^|domain| = 3^(n^2).
keywords.example: For n = 2, 3^4 = 81 functions, the same as |S_1|.

keywords.explain: Two finite sets of the same size admit a bijection, which is simultaneously a surjection and an injection.
keywords.example: Any one-to-one pairing of the 81 matrices with the 81 functions works.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='538' height='218' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='40' class='lbl ink'>|S_1| = 3^(n^2)  (n^2 entries, 3 choices)</text><text x='20' y='80' class='lbl ink'>|S_2| = 3^(n^2)  (n^2 inputs -> {0,1,2})</text><text x='20' y='120' class='lbl hi' style='fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'>|S_1| = |S_2| => bijection exists</text><text x='20' y='160' class='lbl ok'>B (surjection), C (bijection) TRUE</text><text x='20' y='200' class='big no'>A, D FALSE</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Both S_1 and S_2 have cardinality 3^(n^2), so a bijection exists; hence B and C are correct while A and D are false.

given.aim: Compare |S_1| and |S_2| and decide which existence statements about maps between them hold.

terms.meaning: All n x n matrices over the 3-element alphabet {a,b,c}
terms.example: An entry-by-entry filled 2 x 2 grid for n = 2
terms.connects: Has n^2 independent entries, giving |S_1| = 3^(n^2)

terms.meaning: All functions from {0,...,n^2-1} (size n^2) to {0,1,2}
terms.example: A length-n^2 sequence of values in {0,1,2}
terms.connects: Counted by 3^(n^2), matching |S_1|

terms.meaning: Number of matrix entries and number of function inputs
terms.connects: The common exponent making both counts equal

terms.meaning: Types of maps; a bijection is both onto and one-to-one
terms.example: Pair each matrix with a unique function
terms.connects: Equal finite cardinality guarantees all three exist

given.plan: Compute |S_1| = 3^(n^2) and |S_2| = 3^(n^2); since they are equal, a bijection exists, settling each option.

steps.title: Count both sets
steps.apply: An n x n matrix has n^2 entries, each from a 3-element set, so |S_1| = 3^(n^2). A function from an n^2-element domain to a 3-element codomain is counted by 3^(n^2), so |S_2| = 3^(n^2).
steps.note: Both are finite and exactly equal: |S_1| = |S_2| = 3^(n^2).

steps.title: Equal size forces a bijection
steps.formula_raw: |S_1| = |S_2| (finite) => bijection exists => surjection and injection exist
steps.apply: Two finite sets of equal cardinality admit a bijection (option C is TRUE). A bijection is in particular a surjection (option B TRUE) and an injection (so option D, 'no injection', is FALSE; and option A, 'no bijection', is FALSE). Hence the correct choices are B and C.
steps.note: On finite sets a bijection simultaneously witnesses surjectivity and injectivity, knocking out A and D.

solution.result: Since |S_1| = |S_2| = 3^(n^2), a bijection (hence a surjection and an injection) exists, so the correct options are B and C.

questions.formula_note: Both sets count to 3^(n^2); equal finite cardinality guarantees a bijection (bijection-equal-cardinality), which is also a surjection and injection, so B and C hold and A and D fail.

questions.question: Let f: A -> B be an onto (or surjective) function, where A and B are nonempty sets. Define an equivalence relation ~ on the set A as a_1 ~ a_2 if f(a_1) = f(a_2), where a_1, a_2 in A. Let E = {[x] : x in A} be the set of all the equivalence classes under ~. Define a new mapping F: E -> B as F([x]) = f(x), for all the equivalence classes [x] in E. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE? A. F is NOT well-defined. B. F is an onto (or surjective) function. C. F is a one-to-one (or injective) function. D. F is a bijective function.
questions.to_find: Which statements about the induced map F on equivalence classes are TRUE

understand.plain: The relation a_1 ~ a_2 iff f(a_1) = f(a_2) groups inputs by the value they share under f; this is the standard 'kernel' of f. The induced map F sends each class [x] to f(x). First, F is well-defined: if two names [x] = [y] denote the same class then x ~ y, so f(x) = f(y) and F gives the same output regardless of the representative chosen (so A is FALSE). Second, F is one-to-one: F([x]) = F([y]) means f(x) = f(y), which is exactly x ~ y, i.e. [x] = [y]. Third, F is onto because the original f is onto -- every b in B equals f(x) = F([x]) for some x. Onto plus one-to-one means F is a bijection. So B, C and D are all TRUE.

keywords.explain: A map out of a quotient set is well-defined if its value does not depend on which representative of the class you pick.
keywords.example: If [x] = [y] then x ~ y so f(x) = f(y), so F([x]) is unambiguous.

keywords.explain: The relation 'same image under f' partitions the domain into classes, one per attained value of f.
keywords.example: All inputs mapping to b form a single class.

keywords.explain: F collapses each fiber to a point and pairs it with its value; it is injective by construction and onto because f is onto, hence a bijection.
keywords.example: Classes of E line up one-to-one with the elements of B.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='538' height='228' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='110' cy='90' rx='80' ry='70' class='bg'/><text x='75' y='35' class='sub mut'>A (with ~)</text><circle cx='90' cy='70' r='8' class='hi'/><circle cx='130' cy='70' r='8' class='hi'/><circle cx='110' cy='110' r='8' class='ok'/><text x='250' y='60' class='lbl ink'>E = classes</text><rect x='250' y='75' width='90' height='30' class='hi'/><rect x='250' y='115' width='90' height='30' class='ok'/><text x='420' y='60' class='lbl ink'>B</text><circle cx='450' cy='90' r='8' class='hi'/><circle cx='450' cy='130' r='8' class='ok'/><line x1='340' y1='90' x2='442' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='340' y1='130' x2='442' y2='130' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='240' y='200' class='lbl ok'>F: E -> B  is a bijection (B, C, D)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Inputs of A are grouped by shared f-value into classes E; F pairs each class one-to-one with its value in B, an onto map, hence a bijection.

given.aim: Show how F on equivalence classes behaves and identify the true statements among A-D.

terms.meaning: The given onto function defining the relation
terms.example: Every b in B has some preimage
terms.connects: Its surjectivity makes F onto; its values define the classes

terms.meaning: a_1 ~ a_2 iff f(a_1) = f(a_2), the kernel of f
terms.example: Two inputs with the same image are related
terms.connects: Partitions A into fibers of f

terms.meaning: The set of equivalence classes (the quotient A/~)
terms.example: One class per attained value of f
terms.connects: Domain of the induced map F

terms.meaning: The induced map F([x]) = f(x)
terms.example: Class of inputs mapping to b is sent to b
terms.connects: Shown to be well-defined, injective, and surjective

given.plan: Check well-definedness (independence of representative), injectivity (F([x])=F([y]) => [x]=[y]), and surjectivity (from f onto); conclude F is a bijection.

steps.title: F is well-defined and one-to-one
steps.apply: Well-defined: if [x] = [y] then x ~ y, so f(x) = f(y), giving the same value of F no matter which representative is used -- so option A ('NOT well-defined') is FALSE. One-to-one: F([x]) = F([y]) means f(x) = f(y), i.e. x ~ y, i.e. [x] = [y]; hence F is injective, so option C is TRUE.
steps.note: The equivalences chain both ways, which is exactly what makes F a well-defined injection.

steps.title: F is onto, hence a bijection
steps.formula_raw: f onto => every b = f(x) = F([x]) => F onto => F bijective
steps.apply: Since f is onto, every b in B equals f(x) for some x, and then b = F([x]); so F is onto -- option B is TRUE. Being both one-to-one and onto, F is a bijection -- option D is TRUE. Therefore the true statements are B, C and D.
steps.note: F is the canonical bijection from the quotient A/~ onto the image (here all of B, since f is onto).

solution.result: F is well-defined, one-to-one, and onto, hence bijective; the TRUE statements are B, C and D.

questions.formula_note: Quotienting A by the kernel of an onto f yields the canonical bijection F (kernel-quotient-bijection): well-defined and injective by the 'same image' relation, onto because f is onto, so B, C, D are true and A is false.

questions.question: Let A and B be non-empty finite sets such that there exist one-to-one and onto functions (i) from A to B and (ii) from A x A to A union B. The number of possible values of |A| is ___________.
questions.to_find: How many values |A| can take given the two bijection conditions

understand.plain: A one-to-one and onto function between finite sets means they have the same size. Condition (i) gives |A| = |B|; call this m. Condition (ii) gives |A x A| = |A union B|, i.e. m^2 = |A union B|. Now |A union B| lies between max(|A|,|B|) = m (when B = A) and |A| + |B| = 2m (when A and B are disjoint), so m <= m^2 <= 2m. The left inequality m <= m^2 holds for every m >= 1, and the right inequality m^2 <= 2m gives m <= 2. So m can only be 1 or 2 -- both are achievable (m=1 with B=A; m=2 with A,B disjoint). Hence |A| has 2 possible values.

keywords.explain: A one-to-one onto function between finite sets exists exactly when the two sets have the same number of elements.
The Cartesian product of A with itself has |A| x |A| ordered pairs.
For sets of size m each, the union ranges from m (identical sets) to 2m (disjoint sets).
keywords.example: Two 2-element sets union to 2, 3, or 4 elements.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='538' height='228' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='38' class='lbl ink'>(i) bijection A->B => |A| = |B| = m</text><text x='20' y='76' class='lbl ink'>(ii) |A x A| = |A union B| => m^2 = |A union B|</text><text x='20' y='114' class='lbl ink'>m <= |A union B| <= 2m  =>  m <= m^2 <= 2m</text><text x='20' y='150' class='lbl hi' style='fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'>m^2 <= 2m  =>  m <= 2</text><text x='20' y='188' class='lbl ok'>m in {1, 2}</text><text x='20' y='220' class='big ok'>number of values of |A| = 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: From |A|=|B|=m and m^2 = |A union B| with m <= |A union B| <= 2m, we get m <= 2, so |A| in {1,2}: two possible values.

given.aim: Find how many sizes |A| are consistent with both bijection conditions.

terms.meaning: The size of A, the quantity whose possible values we count
terms.connects: Equals |B| by condition (i) and bounds |A union B|

terms.meaning: Size of B, equal to |A| because of the bijection A -> B
terms.connects: Makes |A x A| = m^2 comparable to |A union B|

terms.meaning: Number of ordered pairs from A
terms.connects: Must equal |A union B| by condition (ii)

terms.meaning: Size of the union, between m and 2m
terms.example: m to 2m as A,B overlap or are disjoint
terms.connects: Forces the inequality m <= m^2 <= 2m

given.plan: Translate both bijections into size equalities (|A|=|B|=m and m^2 = |A union B|), bound |A union B| between m and 2m, solve m <= m^2 <= 2m, and count valid m.

steps.title: Turn bijections into size equations
steps.formula_raw: (i) |A| = |B| = m ; (ii) |A x A| = |A union B| => m^2 = |A union B|
steps.apply: A one-to-one onto map between finite sets means equal sizes. From (i), |A| = |B| = m. From (ii), |A x A| = |A union B|; since |A x A| = m^2, we get m^2 = |A union B|.
steps.note: Both conditions are pure cardinality statements once we use bijection => equal size.

steps.title: Bound the union and solve for m
steps.apply: With |A| = |B| = m, the union satisfies m <= |A union B| <= 2m (lower when B = A, upper when A, B disjoint). Substituting m^2 = |A union B| gives m <= m^2 <= 2m. The inequality m <= m^2 holds for all m >= 1; m^2 <= 2m gives m <= 2. So m = 1 (take B = A) or m = 2 (take A, B disjoint), both realizable. Thus |A| has exactly 2 possible values.
steps.note: m = 3 fails: m^2 = 9 > 6 = 2m, so the union can never be that large.

solution.result: The constraints force |A| in {1, 2}, so the number of possible values of |A| is 2.

questions.formula_note: Bijections give |A|=|B|=m and m^2=|A union B| (bijection-equal-cardinality); bounding the union by m <= |A union B| <= 2m forces m^2 <= 2m, i.e. m in {1,2}, so 2 values.

formulas.name: Probability with Equally Likely Outcomes
formulas.plain: P(event) = (number of favorable outcomes) / (total number of equally likely outcomes)
formulas.whenToUse: Use when an object is chosen uniformly at random from a finite sample space and you want the probability of a counted property.

terms.means: the favorable event (set of outcomes with the desired property)
the sample space of all equally likely outcomes

formulas.trap: Only valid when every outcome is equally likely; make sure the denominator counts ALL outcomes (e.g. all functions, not just the favorable ones).

formulas.name: Bijection Exists If and Only If Equal Cardinality (Finite Sets)
formulas.plain: two finite sets admit a bijection exactly when they have the same number of elements; a bijection is also a surjection and an injection
formulas.whenToUse: Use to decide existence of bijections/surjections/injections between finite sets, or to convert 'one-to-one and onto function exists' into an equality of cardinalities.

terms.means: the cardinality (element count) of each set
a one-to-one and onto map, simultaneously a surjection and an injection

formulas.trap: For finite sets equal size guarantees a bijection; for infinite sets 'same cardinality' is subtler and a proper subset can biject with the whole set.
formulas.reference: Cardinality of finite sets, pigeonhole principle

formulas.name: Image Size Bounded by Forced-Equality Classes
formulas.plain: if functional rules force f to take equal values on linked inputs, the number of distinct values f can take is at most the number of equivalence classes those links create
formulas.whenToUse: Use when a function is defined by recurrence/forcing rules like f(n) = f(other(n)); group inputs into classes that must share a value, and count the classes.

terms.means: the function constrained by the forcing rules
the target index that f(n) is forced to equal, f(n) = f(g(n))
equivalence classes of inputs that must share one f-value

formulas.trap: Find an invariant preserved by the rules (here divisibility by 5) to count classes correctly; missing it overcounts or undercounts the distinct values.
formulas.reference: Functional equations, equivalence relations on the domain

formulas.name: Canonical Bijection from the Quotient by f's Kernel onto its Image
formulas.latex: F:[x]\mapsto f(x)\ \text{is a well-defined bijection}\ A/{\sim}\ \to\ \mathrm{Im}(f)
formulas.plain: for the relation a~b iff f(a)=f(b), the induced map F([x]) = f(x) is well-defined and one-to-one; if f is onto, F is an onto bijection onto B
formulas.whenToUse: Use whenever a map is defined on equivalence classes by a representative formula; check it respects the relation (well-defined), then read off injectivity and (from f onto) surjectivity.

terms.means: the kernel relation a~b iff f(a)=f(b) on the domain A
the equivalence class (fiber of f) containing x
the induced map F([x]) = f(x) from classes to B

formulas.trap: Well-definedness must be checked first: the value F([x]) cannot depend on the chosen representative x; it works here precisely because the relation is defined by equal images.
formulas.reference: First isomorphism / fundamental theorem on equivalence relations and functions
